source_intake_record.raw_question_text: An observer to the right of the mirror-lens combination shown in figure (not to scale) sees two real images that are the same size and in the same location. One image is upright, and the other is inverted. Both images are 1.50 times larger than the object. The lens has a focal length of \( 10.0 \text{ cm} \). The lens and mirror are separated by \( 40.0 \text{ cm} \). Determine the focal length of the mirror.

image_dependency: question_text explicitly says 'shown in the figure (not to scale)'
the setup depends on the spatial arrangement of an observer, lens, and mirror, which is typically conveyed by the figure
multi_step: the prompt combines several constraints: two real images, same size, same location, one upright, one inverted
solving requires relating lens focal length, lens-mirror separation, magnification 1.50, and mirror focal length
this is an optical system with interacting components (lens and mirror), indicating multi-stage reasoning rather than a single direct lookup
verifiability: answer_type is short_text with a specific signed numeric value and unit
multiple-choice options are provided, enabling straightforward answer matching
the target quantity is a physics parameter that can be checked by standard lens/mirror equations

decision_reasons: Core assets are complete: question, primary image, and answer are all present and registry_passed=true.
Initial scoring is strong across image dependency, multi-step reasoning, and verifiability, indicating good candidate value.
Risk flags are manageable follow-up concerns rather than missing-core-semantic failures, so they do not justify downgrading or rejection at collection stage.
